annotations.label: nonlinear programming

anchor.snippet: nonlinear programming ( NLP ), also known as nonlinear optimization

annotations.note: No declaration in Mathlib/ defines nonlinear programming or nonlinear optimization as a mathematical object.

annotations.label: optimization problem

anchor.snippet: An optimization problem is one of calculation of the extrema

annotations.note: IsExtrOn formalizes a point being a minimum or maximum of a function on a set, but Mathlib has no OptimizationProblem structure or calculation problem declaration.

annotations.label: nonlinear programming subfield

anchor.snippet: It is the sub-field of mathematical optimization

annotations.note: No declaration in Mathlib/ formalizes subject-area containment such as nonlinear programming being a subfield of mathematical optimization.

annotations.label: nonlinear programming problem setup

anchor.snippet: Let n , m , and p be positive integers.

annotations.note: No declaration in Mathlib/ packages an NLP datum with positive integers n, m, p and associated objective and constraint maps.

annotations.label: nonlinear programming problem form

anchor.snippet: A nonlinear programming problem is an optimization problem of the form

annotations.note: IsMinOn formalizes minimizing an objective over an arbitrary feasible set, but Mathlib has no nonlinear-program form with equality and inequality constraints.

annotations.label: feasible problem

anchor.snippet: feasible problem is one for which there exists at least one set of values

annotations.note: Set.Nonempty exactly expresses existence of a feasible point once constraints are encoded as a set, but Mathlib has no FeasibleProblem declaration.

annotations.label: infeasible problem

anchor.snippet: an infeasible problem is one for which no set of values

annotations.note: Set.not_nonempty_iff_eq_empty identifies no feasible point with an empty feasible set, but Mathlib has no infeasible-problem declaration.

annotations.label: infeasible constraints are contradictory

anchor.snippet: the constraints are mutually contradictory, and no solution exists

annotations.note: Set.not_nonempty_iff_eq_empty formalizes the no-solution part as emptiness of the feasible set, but it does not define mutually contradictory NLP constraints.

annotations.label: unbounded problem

anchor.snippet: unbounded problem is a feasible problem for which the objective function

annotations.note: BddBelow formalizes bounded-below objective images, but Mathlib has no declaration for unbounded optimization problems.

annotations.label: unbounded problem has no optimum

anchor.snippet: there is no optimal solution

annotations.note: For minimization, IsMinOn.bddBelow gives the contrapositive that an objective image unbounded below cannot have an optimum.

annotations.label: feasibility violation handling

anchor.snippet: infeasible problems are handled by minimizing a sum of feasibility violations

annotations.note: No declaration in Mathlib/ formalizes handling infeasible NLPs by minimizing a sum of feasibility violations.

annotations.label: convex nonlinear program

anchor.snippet: then the program is called convex

annotations.note: ConvexOn formalizes convex objective functions on convex sets, but Mathlib has no full convex nonlinear program definition with constraints.

annotations.label: convex optimization methods apply

anchor.snippet: general methods from convex optimization can be used in most cases

annotations.note: IsMinOn.of_isLocalMinOn_of_convexOn captures a key convex-optimization consequence, but Mathlib does not formalize the meta-claim that convex methods apply.

annotations.label: quadratic programming case

anchor.snippet: If the objective function is quadratic and the constraints are linear

annotations.note: QuadraticMap formalizes quadratic maps, but Mathlib has no quadratic programming case definition with linear constraints.

annotations.label: fractional programming transformation

anchor.snippet: then the problem can be transformed to a convex optimization problem

annotations.note: No declaration in Mathlib/ formalizes fractional programming or its transformation to a convex optimization problem.

annotations.label: transportation cost optimization

anchor.snippet: A typical non- convex problem is that of optimizing transportation costs

annotations.note: No declaration in Mathlib/ formalizes transportation cost optimization as a nonlinear programming example.

annotations.label: petroleum product transport

anchor.snippet: An example would be petroleum product transport

annotations.note: No declaration in Mathlib/ formalizes petroleum product transport as an optimization example.

annotations.label: batch size discontinuities

anchor.snippet: the cost functions may have discontinuities

annotations.note: No declaration in Mathlib/ states that batch-size transportation cost functions may be discontinuous.

annotations.label: linear data analysis example

anchor.snippet: some simple data analysis

annotations.note: No declaration in Mathlib/ formalizes the article's simple linear data analysis example.

annotations.label: data fitting is generally nonlinear

anchor.snippet: in general these problems are also nonlinear

annotations.note: No declaration in Mathlib/ states that data-fitting problems are generally nonlinear.

annotations.label: numerical best fit

anchor.snippet: One tries to find a best fit numerically.

annotations.note: Function.argminOn selects an exact minimizer on a nonempty set under a well-founded order, but Mathlib has no numerical best-fit procedure.

annotations.label: KKT necessary conditions

anchor.snippet: the Karush–Kuhn–Tucker (KKT) conditions provide necessary conditions

annotations.note: IsLocalExtrOn.exists_multipliers_of_hasStrictFDerivAt gives equality-constrained Lagrange multiplier necessary conditions, which are only a special case of full KKT inequality and complementarity conditions.

annotations.label: subdifferential KKT conditions

anchor.snippet: subdifferential versions of Karush–Kuhn–Tucker (KKT) conditions are available

annotations.note: Searches found no subdifferential, subgradient, or subdifferential KKT declarations in Mathlib/.

annotations.label: KKT sufficient under convexity

anchor.snippet: the KKT conditions are sufficient for a global optimum

annotations.note: IsMinOn.of_isLocalMinOn_of_convexOn proves a convex local minimum is global, but no declaration states KKT conditions are sufficient under convexity.

annotations.label: KKT local sufficiency without convexity

anchor.snippet: these conditions are sufficient only for a local optimum

annotations.note: No declaration in Mathlib/ establishes KKT local sufficiency without convexity.

annotations.label: finite local optima can be enumerated

anchor.snippet: one can find all of them analytically

annotations.note: No declaration in Mathlib/ states that finitely many local optima can be analytically enumerated.

annotations.label: iterative numerical methods

anchor.snippet: These methods are iterative

annotations.note: No declaration in Mathlib/ defines nonlinear programming numerical methods as iterative procedures.

annotations.label: zero-order routines

anchor.snippet: Zero-order routines - use only the values

annotations.note: No declaration in Mathlib/ defines zero-order optimization routines that use only objective values.

annotations.label: first-order routines

anchor.snippet: First-order routines - use also the values

annotations.note: HasFDerivAt formalizes first derivatives of functions, but Mathlib has no declaration classifying first-order numerical routines.

annotations.label: second-order routines

anchor.snippet: Second-order routines - use also the values

annotations.note: iteratedFDeriv formalizes arbitrary-order derivatives, including second derivatives, but Mathlib has no declaration classifying second-order numerical routines.

annotations.label: higher-order routines not used

anchor.snippet: Third-order routines (and higher) are theoretically possible

annotations.note: iteratedFDeriv supplies higher derivatives in principle, but no declaration states that higher-order optimization routines are unused in practice.

annotations.label: branch and bound method

anchor.snippet: Another method involves the use of branch and bound techniques

annotations.note: No branch-and-bound method declaration was found in Mathlib/.

annotations.label: branch and bound optimality certificate

anchor.snippet: This solution is optimal, although possibly not unique.

annotations.note: No declaration in Mathlib/ formalizes a branch-and-bound optimality certificate.

annotations.label: ε-optimal point

anchor.snippet: such points are called ε-optimal

annotations.note: No declaration in Mathlib/ defines ε-optimal points for optimization problems.

annotations.label: ε-optimal termination

anchor.snippet: Terminating to ε-optimal points is typically necessary

annotations.note: No declaration in Mathlib/ states that branch-and-bound termination to ε-optimal points is necessary.

annotations.label: ALGLIB nonlinear programming solver

anchor.snippet: ALGLIB (C++, C#, Java, Python API) implements several first-order and derivative-free nonlinear programming solvers

annotations.note: No declaration in Mathlib/ records ALGLIB or its nonlinear programming solvers.

annotations.label: NLopt nonlinear programming solver

anchor.snippet: NLopt (C/C++ implementation, with numerous interfaces including Julia, Python, R, MATLAB/Octave), includes various nonlinear programming solvers

annotations.note: No declaration in Mathlib/ records NLopt or its nonlinear programming solvers.

annotations.label: SciPy nonlinear programming solver

anchor.snippet: SciPy (de facto standard for scientific Python) has scipy.optimize solver

annotations.note: No declaration in Mathlib/ records SciPy or scipy.optimize as a nonlinear programming solver.

annotations.label: IPOPT nonlinear programming solver

anchor.snippet: IPOPT (C++ implementation, with numerous interfaces including C, Fortran, Java, AMPL, R, Python, etc.) is an interior point method solver

annotations.note: No declaration in Mathlib/ records IPOPT or its interior-point nonlinear programming solver role.

annotations.label: SNOPT proprietary solver

anchor.snippet: Proprietary solvers include SNOPT

annotations.note: No declaration in Mathlib/ records SNOPT as a proprietary nonlinear programming solver.

annotations.note: No declaration in Mathlib/ formalizes the article's specific two-dimensional nonlinear programming example.
